tax: Loam Creeper is placed at species rank beneath Worked-Earthkin (Argillic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a dark organic-rich loam body, slumping spread along root channels, and a humic smell. It is separated from its sibling the Marl Walker by its dark organic-rich slumping form versus the Walker's pale stiff upright gait; the two share the genus body-plan but never the pale stiff upright gait. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: dark humic loam rich in decayed organic matter, animated by earth-pressure in organic-rich channels.
ap: Loam Creeper stands 1–1.6 m, a low slumping figure. Its silhouette is a rounded low shape of dark moist loam that moves as a slow slide. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a dark grey-brown, almost black surface that smears rather than crumbles; a strong humic smell, like old fungal mat pressed under stone; and a trail of dark smear marks on paler stone where it has passed. It moves with a slow forward slump-and-pull, following root-channel gradients, and shows none; very dark. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a dark smear trail and the smell of old organic matter along its path, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Loam Creeper persists in decaying root-mat channels, organic-rich sediment fills, and peat-like accumulations of the upper tiers where organic chemistry mixes with mineral pressure. It does not breed; it forms where earth-pressure animates an organics-saturated loam accumulation at a root channel, and recurs whenever the root channel re-accumulates organic matter and the loam re-animates. It sustains its mass on earth-pressure in organics-rich loam, drawing in decayed organic matter and clay drawn from the channel to repair. Range is bounded by the organic-rich channel: outside that band the body dries to an inert dark crust away from organic input. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a root channel whose loam accumulation smears against the water-flow rather than with it, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Loam Creeper is roused by disturbance of its root channel or warmth that speeds its organic chemistry and otherwise lies an undisturbed dark loam accumulation. It claims a single root channel or organic accumulation and answers intrusion with slumping forward to engulf and adhere to prey with sticky organic loam. Toward grey-folk it is passive, hard to see in dark passages; survey crews report that Beast-Bonded scouts note when their bond animals refuse to walk a dark accumulation floor. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Loam Creeper: a body of dark humic loam rich in decayed organic matter, animated by earth-pressure in organic-rich channels; immune to piercing and to physical separation, which it just rejoins; and a defining weakness — fire and lime, which destroy the organic chemistry holding it together. Its senses run through the medium itself: organic-gradient and earth-pressure sensing through its root channel. It tolerates darkness, cold, and acid seep but fails at fire and lime contact. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the dark slumping form and humic smell, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Loam Creeper are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it slumps to engulf and adhere with sticky organic loam; it flows along root channels to reach prey in tight gaps; it draws organic matter from the channel to rebuild after damage; and it can adhere to boots and gear to slow and exhaust. Each is a direct expression of earth-pressure in an organics-rich loam body, so it cannot, for instance, jet, conduct, stand upright, or survive fire. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Loam Creeper matters because it makes dark organic-channel floors an adhesion hazard, and bond-animals that refuse a floor are now the accepted field warning. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an adhesion hazard logged with organic-rich sediment channels, and its presence reshapes routes through the upper-tier root channels, now checked by bond-animal reaction before a crew crosses. A root channel whose loam smears against the water-flow marks a Loam Creeper territory.
g: Loam Creeper (Argilla humosa) is a worked-earth elemental of dark organic loam that slumps and spreads along decomposing organic channels in the gorge. Documented from the decaying root-mat and organic-rich sediment channels of the upper tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a dark, organics-rich loam body held coherent by earth-pressure and humic chemistry — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. The Loam Creeper is the dark kin of the Marl Walker: where the Walker is pale and stiff from lime, the Creeper is dark and soft from organic content. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a spreading dark wet smear and a smell of decayed matter where the body collapsed and no carcass.
